title: Model with Arrays
essential_question: How can you use arrays to show multiplication and find products?
strategies: An array has rows and columns

pretest.question: An array has 3 rows of 4. How many in all?

pretest.question: An array has 3 rows of 8. What is 3 × 8?

learn.title: What is an Array?
learn.content: An array is a way to show objects in equal rows and columns. Rows go across (left to right). Columns go up and down. Every row has the same number of objects. Example: 3 rows of 4 dots = 12 dots total.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Reading an Array
learn.content: To read an array: (1) Count the rows — how many rows go across? (2) Count the columns — how many are in each row? (3) Write the multiplication: rows × columns = product. Example: 4 rows of 3 → 4 × 3 = 12.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Arrays and Multiplication Sentences
learn.content: An array with 5 rows and 2 columns shows 5 × 2 = 10. You can also read it as 2 × 5 = 10 (columns × rows). The product is the same either way! Both multiplication sentences describe the same array.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Rotating an Array
learn.content: Rotate a 3×4 array 90° and it becomes a 4×3 array — the product stays 12! This shows the Commutative Property: 3 × 4 = 4 × 3. Arrays prove it visually: same dots, just rearranged in rows and columns.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Arrays in Word Problems
learn.content: Word problems hide arrays. Look for clue words: 'rows' tells you the first factor; 'in each row' or 'columns' tells you the second factor. Example: '5 rows of seats with 4 seats in each row' → draw a 5×4 array → 5 × 4 = 20 seats.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: 행과 열 — 어느 쪽이 가로인가?
learn.content: 약속: 행(row)은 가로(좌→우), 열(column)은 세로(위→아래). 배열 a × b를 읽을 때 — 첫 번째 인자 a = 행의 개수, 두 번째 인자 b = 한 행에 있는 점 개수(=열의 개수). 예: 4 × 6 → 4행 6열 (가로 4개 줄, 각 줄에 6개). 흔한 실수: 행과 열을 뒤바꿈 → 모델 오류 (곱은 같지만 '몇 행?' 물으면 틀림).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: 배열을 90° 회전하면?
learn.content: 3×6 배열을 90° 돌리면 6×3 배열이 됩니다. 점의 개수는 그대로 18개! 이것이 교환법칙(Commutative Property): a × b = b × a. 배열은 이 법칙을 눈으로 보여주는 가장 강력한 도구입니다. 팁: '회전해도 점의 총 개수는 변하지 않는다' — 곱셈의 핵심 직관.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: 배열로 푸는 3가지 전략 요약
learn.content: 전략 1 그리기: 행수만큼 줄을 긋고 각 줄에 열수만큼 점 찍기. 전략 2 곱셈: 행수 × 열수 = 곱(product). 전략 3 인수 찾기: 곱이 주어지면, '? × ? = product' 가능한 행/열 조합 찾기. 예: product=24 → (1,24)(2,12)(3,8)(4,6)(6,4)(8,3)(12,2)(24,1). 검증: 같은 점 개수의 다른 배열은 모두 같은 곱을 갖습니다.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: Make an array: 2 rows of 6. What is the product?
hints: Draw 2 rows. Put 6 dots in each row. Count all the dots.

feedback.incorrect: Draw the array: 2 rows, 6 dots each. Count all: 6, 12. The product is 12.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: A marching band has 4 rows with 7 musicians in each row. How many musicians in all?
hints: '4 rows' is your first factor. '7 musicians in each row' is your second factor.

feedback.incorrect: 4 rows, 7 per row → 4 × 7 = 28. Think of an array with 4 rows and 7 columns.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: An array shows 3 × 4 = 12. If you add one more row of 4, what is the new product?
try.explanation: Adding 1 more row makes 4 rows of 4. 4 × 4 = 16.
hints: The original array is 3 rows of 4. Adding 1 row means 3 + 1 = 4 rows of 4.

feedback.incorrect: Start with 3 rows of 4. Add 1 more row: now 4 rows of 4. 4 × 4 = 16.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: An array has 3 rows of 8. What is the product? Enter the number.
hints: Count the rows (3) and the number in each row (8).

feedback.correct: Yes! 3 × 8 = 24. Your array has 24 total objects.
feedback.incorrect: 3 rows of 8: skip-count by 8 three times — 8, 16, 24. The product is 24.

try.feedback_correct: Yes! 3 × 8 = 24. Your array has 24 total objects.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: Which array shows 2 × 9?
hints: In 2 × 9, the first factor (2) tells you the number of rows.
The second factor (9) tells you how many are in each row — that is the number of columns.

feedback.incorrect: First factor = rows. Second factor = columns. So 2 × 9 → 2 rows, 9 columns.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: 6 rows of 2 means 6 groups with 2 in each.

feedback.incorrect: 6 rows, 2 per row. Skip-count by 2 six times: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 = 12.

practice_r1.explanation: 3 × 7 means the first factor (3) = rows, second factor (7) = columns.
hints: In a multiplication sentence, the first factor tells you the rows.

feedback.incorrect: First factor = rows, second factor = columns. So 3 × 7 → 3 rows, 7 columns.

hints: 2 rows, 9 columns → write it as 2 × 9. Then find the product.

feedback.incorrect: rows × columns gives the sentence. 2 rows × 9 columns = 2 × 9 = 18.

Then take away 1 full row of 5: 30 − 5 = ?

practice_r2.question: An array has some rows of 8 and the product is 40. How many rows?
hints: You know columns (8) and product (40). Find the rows.
Think: how many groups of 8 make 40? Count by 8: 8, 16, 24, 32, 40 — that's 5 groups.

feedback.incorrect: Count by 8 until you reach 40: 8, 16, 24, 32, 40 — five jumps = 5 rows.

practice_r2.question: Array A: 3 rows of 7. Array B: 7 rows of 3. Which statement is true?
choices: A. Array A has a larger product
B. Array B has a larger product
C. Both arrays have the same product
D. The products cannot be compared
practice_r2.explanation: 3×7=21 and 7×3=21. Rotating an array does not change the product.
hints: Remember the Commutative Property: changing the order of factors does not change the product.

feedback.correct: Right! 3×7=21 and 7×3=21. Rotating an array keeps the same product.
feedback.incorrect: 3×7=21 and 7×3=21. The order of factors doesn't matter — same product!

practice_r2.feedback_correct: Right! 3×7=21 and 7×3=21. Rotating an array keeps the same product.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

practice_r2.question: Find the difference: 905 − 348.
practice_r2.explanation: 905−348: 5−8 borrow → 15−8=7; tens 0−4 borrow → 10−5=5 (after lending); hundreds 8−3=5. Result: 557.
hints: Borrow across the zero in the tens.

practice_r3.question: Jenny arranges 24 chairs in equal rows. Which arrangement is NOT possible?
hints: Multiply each option and check if the product equals 24.

feedback.correct: Correct! 5×5=25≠24. That arrangement is NOT possible.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Correct! 5×5=25≠24. That arrangement is NOT possible.

practice_r3.question: Array 1: 3 rows of 9. Array 2: 9 rows of 3. Are the products the same?
practice_r3.explanation: 3×9=27 and 9×3=27. The Commutative Property: rows×cols = cols×rows.
hints: When you swap rows and columns, the product stays the same.

feedback.correct: Right! 3×9=27 and 9×3=27. Arrays prove the Commutative Property.
feedback.incorrect: 3×9=27 and 9×3=27 — same product. Rotating the array doesn't change the total.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Right! 3×9=27 and 9×3=27. Arrays prove the Commutative Property.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

practice_r3.question: A theater has 8 rows of 9 seats. The first 3 rows are reserved. How many seats are available?
hints: Step 2: Find reserved seats: 3 rows × 9 = 27. Subtract from total: 72 − 27 = ?

feedback.correct: Great multi-step thinking! 8×9=72, minus 3×9=27, gives 45 available seats.
feedback.incorrect: Total = 8×9=72. Reserved = 3×9=27. Available = 72−27=45. Answer is A.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Great multi-step thinking! 8×9=72, minus 3×9=27, gives 45 available seats.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.3 Lesson 3.5 'Model with Arrays' pp.119-122
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Multiplication and Arrays
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

practice_r3.question: Which array does NOT have a product of 18?
hints: Multiply each option and find which one does NOT equal 18.

feedback.correct: Correct! 4×5=20≠18. That array does NOT have a product of 18.

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Correct! 4×5=20≠18. That array does NOT have a product of 18.
